one_line_takeaway: Masking cross-attention between retrieved documents at inference time (SDAG) is a training-free, model-architecture-preserving defense that statistically significantly reduces RAG corpus-poisoning attack success rate and improves accuracy versus standard causal attention and prior filtering-based defenses (RAGDefender, Discern&Answer), and composes with those defenses for further gains -- but it requires white-box attention-mask control, restricting it to open-source LLMs.
key_finding: In the single-adversarial-document setting (Table 1), SDAG statistically significantly outperforms CARG on both ACC and ASR across nearly all generator/retriever/k/dataset combinations, e.g. for Llama+E5, k=5, NQ: SDAG ACC 0.37 vs CARG ACC 0.17 (ASR 0.33 vs 0.41); for Mistral+Contriever, k=5, TriviaQA: SDAG ACC 0.47/ASR 0.53 vs CARG ACC 0.40/ASR 0.75. Across attack strategies on NQ (Table 2, E5, k=5), SDAG consistently gives higher ACC and statistically significantly lower ASR than CARG for Random, Near, and Far strategies alike (e.g., Mistral: SDAG ASR 0.26/0.33/0.23 vs CARG ASR 0.55/0.56/0.53 for Random/Near/Far respectively); the Far strategy is consistently the least effective attack for both methods, supporting the paper's embedding-space analysis that adversarial documents closer to the benign-document centroid are more effective attacks. Against defense baselines (Table 3, Llama+E5, single-document setting), SDAG outperforms CARG, Discern&Answer, and RAGDefender in most comparisons (e.g., NQ: SDAG ACC 0.37/ASR 0.17 vs CARG 0.33/0.41, D&A 0.34/0.38, RAGDefender 0.28/0.33); the paper reports RAGDefender in particular degrades sharply from single- to multiple-document settings because it relies on similarity among adversarial documents. Integrating SDAG with existing pre-generation defenses (SDAG-D&A, SDAG-RAGDefender) statistically significantly improves ASR over the standalone baseline in all reported cases and establishes what the paper calls a new state-of-the-art in the multiple-document setting.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Sparse Document Attention RAG (SDAG), an inference-time defense against corpus knowledge poisoning attacks on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. SDAG replaces the standard causal attention mechanism of decoder-only LLMs with a block-sparse attention mask that disallows cross-attention between tokens belonging to different retrieved documents (tokens within a document, and task-instruction/context tokens, retain standard causal attention). The method requires no fine-tuning and no architectural change to the underlying LLM -- only a modification to the attention mask applied at inference time -- so it can be combined with any pre-generation defense (e.g., document filtering methods) rather than replacing them.
threat_model: Corpus knowledge poisoning attack: an attacker injects adversarial documents containing deliberately crafted misinformation into the RAG corpus (or directly into the retrieved set) to steer the generator's answer toward an attacker-chosen target answer. Two settings are studied: 'in-corpus' (attacker injects N adversarial documents into the full corpus C, and the retriever may or may not surface them) and 'in-set' (adversarial documents are assumed already present in the retrieved set D, isolating the generation-stage effect). The paper explicitly excludes 'black hat' attacks aimed at disabling the system rather than steering its output. Three adversarial-document selection strategies are defined: Random (uniform sampling from an LLM-generated candidate pool), Near (documents closest to the centroid of benign retrieved documents in embedding space), and Far (documents farthest from that centroid) -- the paper states Near and Far are, to its authors' knowledge, novel to this study. The paper studies both single-adversarial-document attacks (a scenario it argues is under-explored in prior RAG-defense literature and can defeat similarity-based defenses) and multiple-document attacks (2 or 3 adversarial documents).
limitations: The paper's own scope restrictions: (1) SDAG requires white-box control of the attention mask, limiting evaluation to open-source 7-8B generators (Llama-8B, Qwen-7B, Mistral-7B); it cannot be applied to closed-source API models such as GPT-4o. (2) The main results use temperature 0.1 and 7-8B-scale models; effects of temperature, model size, and reasoning capability are relegated to an appendix, described only as 'in line with' the main results rather than reported in the reviewed body text. (3) Detection is not performed -- SDAG structurally prevents adversarial documents from influencing benign-document representations but does not identify or remove them, so accuracy gains are sometimes decoupled from ASR gains (the paper notes explicitly that reducing ASR does not necessarily translate into corresponding accuracy improvements, i.e., SDAG can shift outputs away from the attacker's target answer without landing on the correct answer). (4) RAGDefender's degradation from single- to multi-document settings is a limitation of the baseline the paper highlights, not of SDAG itself, but it also means the paper's strongest 'new state-of-the-art' claim for multi-document settings rests on integrating SDAG with a baseline that the paper itself shows is comparatively weak in that regime. (5) Corpus/threat model excludes 'black hat' (denial/disruption) attacks and RAG variants that incorporate documents after an initial generation phase, which the paper explicitly places out of scope.

datasets_used: Natural Questions / NQ (single-hop QA, 1,000 sampled validation questions)
English Wikipedia dump (December 20, 2018), split into 21,015,324 non-overlapping 100-word passages, used as the retrieval corpus
quantitative_results: Table 1, Llama+E5, k=5, NQ: SDAG ACC 0.37 / ASR 0.17 vs CARG ACC 0.17 / ASR 0.41
Table 1, Mistral+Contriever, k=5, TriviaQA: SDAG ACC 0.47 / ASR 0.53 vs CARG ACC 0.40 / ASR 0.75
Table 1, Qwen+Contriever, k=5, HotpotQA: SDAG ACC 0.10 / ASR 0.67 vs CARG ACC 0.07 / ASR 0.81
Table 2, NQ, E5, k=5, Mistral generator: Random attack SDAG ASR 0.26 vs CARG ASR 0.55; Far attack SDAG ASR 0.23 vs CARG ASR 0.53
Table 3, Llama+E5, single-document, NQ: SDAG ACC 0.37/ASR 0.17 vs CARG 0.33/0.41, Discern&Answer 0.34/0.38, RAGDefender 0.28/0.33
Table 3, Llama+E5, single-document, HotpotQA: SDAG ACC 0.23/ASR 0.41 vs CARG 0.15/0.68, Discern&Answer 0.16/0.64, RAGDefender 0.10/0.70
Adversarial document generation pool: 5 distinct adversarial documents per target answer generated via PoisonedRAG framework using GPT-4o at sampling temperature 1
baselines_compared: CARG (Causal Attention RAG -- standard, unmodified causal attention; SDAG's architectural counterpart)
RAGDefender (Kim et al., 2025) -- lightweight post-retrieval, pre-generation filtering of adversarial documents based on document similarity
Discern&Answer (Hong et al., 2024) -- GPT-based method that leverages LLM discriminative capability to identify and prioritize reliable evidence among retrieved documents
SDAG combined with RAGDefender and SDAG combined with Discern&Answer (integration variants, not standalone baselines)
